title: Why You Overreact to Small Things and Underreact to Big Ones
researcherTopic: structural dissociation, healing the fragmented self
metaDescription: Explore why complex trauma leads to overreacting to small things and underreacting to big ones. Learn how structural dissociation shapes your responses.
imageDescription: A delicate ceramic bowl, repaired with shimmering gold in the kintsugi style, rests on a rustic wooden surface. Warm, gentle morning light illuminates the intricate golden cracks, symbolizing the beauty and strength found in healing emotional wounds. The scene evokes a sense of peace, resilience, and the profound value of integration.
htmlContent:
I've sat with hundreds of people as they describe the bewildering experience of emotional dysregulation—the way a seemingly minor inconvenience can trigger a tidal wave of panic, anger, or despair, while a genuinely significant crisis is met with a strange, hollow calm. This landscape of disproportionate reactions is not a personal failing or a sign of being "too sensitive"; it is the coherent, logical outcome of a nervous system shaped by the impossible demands of complex trauma. We learn to navigate a world that feels perpetually unsafe by developing sophisticated, albeit costly, survival strategies that calibrate our responses not to the present moment, but to the echoes of a past that is not yet over. The result is a life lived in a state of constant, low-grade predictive error, where the smoke alarm shrieks at a piece of burnt toast while the house quietly burns down around us.

The Architecture of Dissociation

To understand why we overreact to the small things and underreact to the big ones, we must first appreciate the architecture of a nervous system under siege. When trauma is not a single event but an environment—a chronic state of danger, neglect, or unpredictability—the psyche adapts by fragmenting. This is not a metaphorical shattering, but a neurobiological reality. As trauma researcher Janina Fisher explains, we develop different "parts" of ourselves to handle the overwhelming experiences. There are parts that hold the terror, the shame, and the pain of the trauma, and there are parts that try to get on with the business of living, of going to school, of holding down a job, of appearing "normal." This is the foundation of what Fisher calls structural dissociation, a brilliant survival strategy that allows us to function by walling off the unmanageable material. The problem is that the walls are porous, and they are expensive to maintain.

The "apparently normal part" of us—the one that pays the bills and makes small talk—operates by avoiding anything that might activate the "emotional parts" holding the trauma. This requires a tremendous amount of energy, a constant scanning of the environment for potential threats. A partner's slightly off tone of voice, a missed email, the sound of a car backfiring—these are the small things that can slip through the cracks in the wall, activating the hibernating emotional parts and unleashing a disproportionate flood of terror or rage. It feels like an overreaction because the conscious, "normal" part of us has no context for the intensity of the feeling. It doesn’t understand that it’s not reacting to the burnt toast, but to a lifetime of feeling unseen, unheard, and unsafe. The system is not broken; it is doing exactly what it was designed to do, which is to scream "danger" at the first sign of a familiar threat pattern, no matter how faint.

There is no version of growth that doesn't involve the dissolution of something you thought was permanent.

The Logic of Underreaction

If overreaction is the system screaming "danger," then underreaction is the system playing dead. When faced with a genuinely significant crisis—a job loss, a health diagnosis, a major betrayal—the sheer scale of the threat can be so overwhelming that it triggers a different kind of dissociative response. Instead of a flood of emotion, there is an eerie absence of it. This is the freeze response, the oldest survival mechanism in our evolutionary toolkit. The body and mind go numb, a state of detached, foggy calm that can be deeply unsettling to experience and to witness. It’s a state I’ve seen many clients describe as feeling like they are watching a movie of their own life, a spectator to their own catastrophe. This is not a choice. It is an automatic, physiological shutdown designed to conserve energy and minimize pain when fight or flight are not possible.

This underreaction is not a sign of strength or resilience in the way our culture often imagines. It is the nervous system pulling the emergency brake, a radical act of self-preservation that comes at a high cost. While it may get us through the immediate crisis, the unprocessed emotional and physiological energy of the event gets stored in the body, contributing to the very backlog of trauma that fuels our overreactions to smaller stressors. We underreact to the big things by going numb, and that numbness perpetuates the hypervigilance that makes us overreact to the small things. It is a self-perpetuating cycle, a feedback loop between too much and not enough, all of it orchestrated by a nervous system doing its absolute best to keep us alive in a world it has learned to perceive as fundamentally unsafe. For more on this, you might find our article on the body's memory helpful.

The Tyranny of the Apparently Normal Part

The part of us that strives for normalcy, the one that is so often praised for its resilience and high-functioning capacity, can become a tyrant. It is the part that judges our overreactions, that shames our underreactions, and that desperately tries to maintain control in a system that is, by its very nature, out of control. This internal manager believes that if it can just think its way through the problem, if it can just enforce enough rules and maintain enough vigilance, it can prevent the messy, chaotic emotional parts from ever surfacing again. It is the part of us that reads self-help books and tries to implement morning routines, that intellectualizes feelings and seeks logical explanations for the illogical experience of trauma. It is the part that is reading this article right now, hoping to find the key, the answer, the one thing that will finally make it all make sense.

For further reading and professional resources, visit the National Alliance on Mental Illness.

But the manager’s attempts to control the system are precisely what perpetuate the cycle. By judging and suppressing the emotional parts, it only reinforces their sense of being bad, wrong, and dangerous. It is a form of internal warfare, a constant battle for control that exhausts our resources and keeps us trapped in the very patterns we are trying to escape. The work of healing is not to strengthen the manager, but to cultivate a different kind of internal leadership—one that is not based on control, but on compassion, curiosity, and connection. This is the work of what the Internal Family Systems (IFS) model calls the Self, a core of calm, confident, and compassionate presence that exists within every one of us, no matter how fragmented we have become. The goal is not to get rid of the parts, but to get to know them, to understand their roles, and to unburden them from the extreme beliefs and emotions they have been carrying for so long.

When you stop trying to fix the moment, something remarkable happens — the moment becomes workable.

Cultivating the Middle Ground

The path out of this oscillation between over- and underreaction is not a straight line, but a slow, patient process of widening the window of tolerance. It is the practice of learning to stay present with our internal experience without becoming overwhelmed by it or shutting down to it. This is not something we can think our way into. It is something we must feel our way into, one sensation at a time. It begins with the simple, radical act of noticing. Noticing the tightness in the chest, the heat in the face, the clenching in the jaw. Noticing the impulse to run, to fight, to freeze. Noticing the stories the mind tells about these sensations, the judgments it heaps upon them. And then, in the space of that noticing, introducing a sliver of curiosity. What is this sensation? Where did it come from? What does it need?

This is the work of somatic inquiry, of learning the language of the body. It is the work of practices like meditation, breathwork, and gentle movement, which help us to develop a more intimate and compassionate relationship with our own nervous system. It is the work of learning to pendulate, to touch into the difficult sensations and then return to a place of relative safety and resource within ourselves. This is not about eliminating the reactions, but about changing our relationship to them. It is about learning that we can be the container for our experience, rather than being consumed by it. A client once described this to me as learning to be the sky instead of the weather—to recognize that the storms of emotion can pass through without destroying the vast, open expanse of awareness that holds them. If you're interested in exploring this further, our article on somatic experiencing might be a good next step.

The Role of Co-Regulation

We cannot do this work alone. The nervous system is not a closed loop; it is an open, relational system that is exquisitely attuned to the state of the nervous systems around it. We are wounded in relationship, and we heal in relationship. This is the principle of co-regulation, the process by which one nervous system helps to calm and regulate another. For those of us who grew up in environments where our caregivers were a source of threat rather than safety, the capacity for self-regulation is often underdeveloped. We need the presence of a safe, attuned other—a therapist, a trusted friend, a partner—to help us navigate the treacherous waters of our own internal world. This is why so much of the healing journey happens in the context of a therapeutic relationship, as described in many articles on sites like Psychology Today.

A good therapist provides a nervous system that is regulated enough to act as an anchor for our own. They offer the non-judgmental presence, the compassionate curiosity, and the unwavering belief in our capacity to heal that we may never have received as children. In their presence, we can begin to approach the parts of ourselves that we have long feared and exiled. We can learn to listen to their stories, to feel their pain, and to offer them the comfort and care they have always needed. This is not about being saved by another person. It is about borrowing their regulatory capacity until we can cultivate our own. It is about experiencing, in a felt, embodied way, what it is like to be safe in the presence of another, and then slowly, over time, learning to offer that same sense of safety to ourselves. For those seeking to understand the different parts of themselves, our article on Internal Family Systems can be a valuable resource.

The self you're trying to improve is the same self doing the improving. Notice the circularity.

The Long Road of Integration

There is no quick fix for a nervous system shaped by complex trauma. The work of integration is a long, slow, and often nonlinear process. It is a journey of reclaiming the lost parts of ourselves, of grieving the childhood we never had, and of slowly, painstakingly building a life that feels like our own. There will be moments of profound insight and connection, and there will be moments of deep despair and regression. There will be times when we feel like we are finally getting it, and there will be times when we feel like we are right back where we started. This is the nature of the work. It is not a ladder we climb, but a spiral we walk, returning to the same core themes again and again, each time with a little more awareness, a little more compassion, and a little more capacity to stay present with what is.

The goal is not to arrive at some mythical state of being "healed," a place where we no longer have triggers or difficult emotions. The goal is to become more intimate with the full spectrum of our human experience, to learn to navigate our internal world with skill and with grace, and to build a life that is rich and meaningful, not in spite of our history, but because of it. It is to understand that our overreactions and our underreactions are not signs of our brokenness, but of our resilience. They are the echoes of a younger self who did whatever it took to survive. The work of healing is not to silence that self, but to finally, after all this time, welcome it home. And for those on this path, a link to explore further consciousness work can be found at the path to embodied awareness.

You have read the information. You understand the mechanisms. You can explain the theory to someone else. None of that matters if you do not put your body where your understanding is. Knowledge without embodiment is just another defense mechanism. A sophisticated one, but a defense mechanism nonetheless.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why do I get so angry over little things?

This is often a sign that your nervous system is carrying a significant backlog of unprocessed fight energy. When you couldn't fight back against the original source of threat, that survival energy gets stored. A minor annoyance in the present can then act as a release valve for a lifetime of stored rage, leading to a reaction that feels disproportionate to the current event.

Is it bad that I don't feel anything during a crisis?

This numbness, or dissociation, is not bad; it's a highly intelligent survival response. When a situation is too overwhelming for your system to handle, the freeze response kicks in, creating emotional and physical distance from the event to help you endure it. The key is to find ways to gently thaw and process the feelings later, in a safe environment, so they don't remain stuck.

How can I start to feel more balanced?

The journey toward balance begins with small moments of mindful attention to your body. Instead of trying to change how you feel, simply notice the sensations present—tightness, heat, tingling—without judgment. This practice, over time, builds your capacity to stay present with your experience, which is the foundation of nervous system regulation and emotional balance.
